body:
It is anticipated that Python 3.15 will support a version of the Limited API that means the same extension will be importable in both regular and free-threading Python. Some of the details are still to be decided but Cython aims to support this.

To let you start testing this early I've pushed a [`freethreading-limited-api-preview`](https://github.com/cython/cython/tree/freethreading-limited-api-preview) branch to this repo. This branch is intended to be fairly short-lived and will be deleted once it's all merge into the repo.  The branch will probably not be kept rigorously up-to-date with other Cython bug fixes

It's a merge of the following PRs and branches:

- [ ] https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7382
- [x] https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7190
- [ ] https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7333
- [ ] https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7412
- [ ] https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7410
- [ ] https://github.com/da-woods/cython/tree/cdef-class-opaque-pyobject
- [ ] Cherry-pick e03bc1095 (to allow marking a module as freethreading_compatible)
- [ ] Some memoryview-handling stuff (probably to be picked separately) e.g. 87d9cea4d8e6d, 6d0af5b6dda0a,
- [ ] Extern types 2c86e5fa2 (probably more)

Be aware that it is missing some some bits of internal thread safety which would be provided by critical sections and PyMutexes since these have not yet been added to the Limited API.

# To use

Define the following `CFLAGS` when you build:

```
-DPy_LIMITED_API=0x030F0000 -DCYTHON_OPAQUE_OBJECTS=1 -D_Py_OPAQUE_PYOBJECT
```

Build with as recent alpha of Python 3.15 as you can.

Set up your build system normally so that the modules are named `abi3.so` (see https://cython.readthedocs.io/en/latest/src/userguide/limited_api.html#building-with-the-limited-api for some hints)

# Known Limitations

Anything involving an extern type (`cdef extern class` or similar) is unlikely to work.

Performance is about 13% slower compare with the `Py_LIMITED_API=0x030E0000` - essentially every access to an attribute of a cdef class goes through a painful indirect lookup.

Some thread-safety guards missing right now.

comment_data.body: One detail that I've subsequently spotted is that it breaks our memoryview ABI compatibility. That's going to need either some workarounds or some checks.

The other outstanding issue is access to cdef classes without the GIL. I've currently implemented a very hacky workaround, but there's no guarantee it'll keep working long-term and Python developers are sceptical of it.

I've noticed that the generated C file has:

```c
  #if CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_CPYTHON_FREETHREADING
  {Py_mod_gil, Py_MOD_GIL_NOT_USED},
  #endif
```

rather than:

```c
  #if CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_CPYTHON_FREETHREADING || CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_LIMITED_API_FREETHREADING
  {Py_mod_gil, Py_MOD_GIL_NOT_USED},
  #endif
```

Is this intentional?

I've pushed a mid-sized update to this.

* fixes a few bugs with vtab access
* more importantly, guards a hack related to attribute lookup without the GIL. The Python core-devs weren't hugely happy about the approach (this is fine... it's the natural equilibrium for Python core-devs to be unhappy at hacks in Cython) I'd taken because it may not be long-term stable. I've now at least got a lower-performance fallback ready if it the underlying assumption ends up changing.

I'll probably push another update later this week to try to clean up some memoryview stuff, time permitting. That'll be more code quality rather than any change in what works.

Over in https://github.com/cython/cython/pull/7476#issuecomment-3786577880, @da-woods pointed out that defining wrappers for PyObject subtypes like NumPy does in its Cython headers will need some work on the Cython side.

NumPy currently does something like this:

```
cdef extern from "numpy/arrayscalars.h":

    # abstract types
    ctypedef class numpy.generic [object PyObject]:
        pass
```

Maybe NumPy's cython headers will need some updates?

I've pushed some improvements to the handling of extern types (e.g. numpy's `numpy.ufuncobject`). You can now specify:

```
ctypedef class numpy.generic [object PyObject, opaque_pyobject False]:  # or true
```

to explicitly specify whether the extern type opaque or not. It defaults to `False` so there's no need to specify it for existing code.
This means that non-opaque Cython modules can use opaque extern types (at least partly).

Opaque Cython modules don't check the size of non-opaque extern types. Largely because it will definitely be a compile error if you try to access their attributes, so it's "relatively" safe.

I imagine some of this will end up changing, so don't rely too much on it in this exact form. But hopefully it lets people get a bit further with the Numpy headers.

@da-woods I'm planning to add a section to PEP 803 that quotes ecosystem library maintainers to comment on why PEP 803 will be useful. Can you comment a bit about why it'll be beneficial for Cython users? I can then excerpt that for the PEP.

No worries if you're not comfortable doing that.

@ngoldbaum I don't mind being quoted but.... I don't _fully_ understand the appeal of the Stable ABI.

Cython is doesn't have huge problems with the number of wheels we distribute because ultimately it works fine as pure-Python. We do distribute wheels for a few of the smaller platforms as Stable ABI wheels but that's more "dogfooding" than because we actually need to. So I'm adding this in anticipation that other people will find it useful rather than because I will.

I do remain slightly concerned that the performance trade-offs for this will turn out to be too much for many Cython users (it's possible that the trade-off may be different for other binding tools). That's not a huge disaster since we're not getting rid of the regular compilation mode so people are free to pick their own personal trade-offs.

But none of that makes a particularly useful quote...
I think it was useful! I opened https://github.com/python/peps/pull/4831.

> I do remain slightly concerned that the performance trade-offs for this will turn out to be too much for many Cython users

Our benchmarks report our Limited API code (the `L-` columns) as visibly slower than our optimised CPython code, e.g.

https://github.com/cython/cython/actions/runs/22303110980

Most of the big-gap ones are micro-benchmarks, but from the rest, especially object intensive benchmarks like "richards" suffer by factors. They are often slower than the non-compiled pure Python code (the column right of it). The proposed Stable ABI changes are likely to make this relation even worse, and thus completely useless for anything performance relevant. [EDIT: I was referring to code where the performance of the C-API code matters, not one-shot long running native data processing where it does not.]

I can see the "external library wrapping" use case still being a candidate for the Stable ABI, even with the new worse performance. There are certainly cases where people say "I need the functionality, regardless of the speed". PyQT comes to my mind as a reasonable example, also audio/video processing libraries etc. that just need a "configure and run". There are certainly cases like this. But in many such cases, running a subprocess will be hugely easier and faster than connecting to a library through the Limited API.

I can't say whether it's really worth building and supporting a whole second API construct just for this use case. Performance matters for a lot of users in the Python ecosystem, and this isn't just about some "~10% YMMV". Anything for which the wrapping performance matters will probably try hard to avoid the Stable ABI at all cost.

> The proposed Stable ABI changes are likely to make this relation even worse, and thus completely useless for anything performance relevant.

I am not sure this take is correct in general. It seems to be true when one is using lots of short, micro-second level operations. When working with larger array data (quite common for numerical/scientific libraries), the differences quickly seem to converge to <=10%. See for example benchmarks for PyWavelets (which uses a lot of Cython code, and some C code) in https://github.com/PyWavelets/pywt/pull/828. Scikit-learn (which is even more heavy on Cython usage) benchmarks are in the works, I'd expect roughly similar outcomes.

My expectation is that for the most popular platforms, well-maintained scientific libraries are likely to continue to take the wheel building pain to avoid any performance regressions, but for niche platforms it'll be either "no wheels" or "stable ABI wheels". And for less well-maintained libraries, the tradeoffs are leaning even more towards the stable ABI being useful.

I agree with Ralph, but I want to add an asterisk there:

> for less well-maintained libraries*, the tradeoffs are leaning even more towards the stable ABI being useful.

*: this is also true for libraries that are very well maintained but *finished*. I have a couple small libraries that evolve so slowly that needing (essentially identical) new binaries once a year *dominates* their release cycle, and while I'm certainly interested in making them free-threading compatible, I don't want to double or triple the number of wheels I need to make just for this (still niche) target. Having a single target I could compile to and support both builds would be a game changer, and I would happily provide free-threading compatible binaries in these conditions. The performance in these libraries did not suffer *at all* from the switch to abi3, as far as I could tell; they only ever deal with large-ish arrays, with long-running hot loops and very little to no interaction with the Python runtime.

@da-woods I'd like to get your opinion on something. Is it perhaps problematic that opaque PyObject builds are "viral"? I was just testing [cymem](https://github.com/explosion/cymem) since it has few dependencies and a nontrivial use of `cython.critical_section`. I noticed this when I tried to use the cymem thread safety test that Lysandros came up with when we were working on free-threaded support in the explosion stack earlier this year: https://github.com/lysnikolaou/test-cymem-threadsafety.

```
    def test_concurrent_pool_operations():
>       from test_cymem_threadsafety.test_pool import test_concurrent_pool_operations

../../../../../tests/test_runner.py:2:
_ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _ _

>   ???
E   TypeError: Cimported type 'type' has '__cython_typeoffset__'. You are probably trying to cimport a module built with CYTHON_OPAQUE_OBJECTS set from a module without it set.

test_pool.pyx:1: TypeError
```

Here I'm building the `test-cymem-threadsafety` project with a "normal" cython build and no opaque PyObject layout.

I think that means projects who ship Cython headers will need to be very careful about shipping abi3t wheels, since it will cause downstream build breakage for anyone using the Cython headers.

Can this restriction be relaxed perhaps? If not, what do you think the guidance should be for projects that ship Cython headers? I think that includes SciPy.

> I think that includes SciPy.

Just quickly looking at Scipy, I think the pxd files there are mostly functional, and don't really have `cdef classes`? If that is indeed the case, then you should be fine.

---------------------------------------

More generally:

It won't be possible to cimport a non-opaque cdef class into an opaque module. Just because you'll have no way of accessing the members.

With a bit of work, it should be possible to cimport an opaque cdef class into a non-opaque module.  The main thing we'd need to add is some way of labelling classes (or more likely modules) as opaque at compile-time. If we know that then we can ensure that all members are looked up the opaque way.

(Not directly related to freethreading or this version of the Stable ABI, but there might be some value in letting users force classes to use opaque pyobject even if the rest of the module isn't. Mainly that it allows inheritance from builtin types when using the Stable ABI.  I don't know how valuable that is though since no-one's complained about it yet).

The awkward thing is that "opaque pyobject" is set with C-compile-time flags (and realistically, people might well build and distribute versions of the same thing both with and without it). However, to handle the "opaque class cimported into non-opaque module" case, Cython would probably want to know at Cython-compile-time. I don't know what the best way to handle that is.

I'll think about it some more though....

-----------------------------------------

There's also a big incompatibility with passing typed memoryviews between opaque/non-opaque module interface. I believe that should be solveable though and so I'm working on it. (It's also good to do generally because it solves a problem where we've inadvertently made typed memoryviews very hard to change)
SciPy's public Cython API (= `cython_blas`, `cython_lapack`, `cython_special`) should be functions only indeed.

> With a bit of work, it should be possible to cimport an opaque cdef class into a non-opaque module.

I think that would solve the virality issue I was worried about, sounds good.

title: [INFO] Limited API and freethreading Python 3.15
